```python
def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

```

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

```

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names

          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)

          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule.

          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]

          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule.

          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

```

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

```

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
        '{"participants": ["Alice", "Bob"], "duration_minutes": 30, "working_hours": ["9:00", "17:00"], "days": ["Monday", "Tuesday"], "preference": "earliest", "schedules": {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00", "10:00"], ["15:00", "23:59"]], "Tuesday": []}, "Bob": {"Monday": [], "Tuesday": [["0:00", "23:59"]]}}}'
    """

def parse_schedules(problem_text: str) -> str:
    """Parse a calendar scheduling problem into structured JSON.

    Args:
        problem_text: The ENTIRE scheduling problem description as a single
            string. Pass the whole problem text (participants, their busy
            times, duration, preferences, working hours, etc.) as one
            argument — do NOT split it into multiple arguments.

    Returns:
        A JSON string encoding a dict with these keys:
        - participants: list of participant names
        - duration_minutes: meeting duration (30 or 60)
        - working_hours: [start_str, end_str] e.g. ["9:00", "17:00"]
        - days: list of day names e.g. ["Monday", "Tuesday"]
        - preference: "earliest" or "latest" or null (default to null if no explicit preference is stated)
        - schedules: dict mapping participant name to dict mapping day to
          list of busy intervals e.g. {"Alice": {"Monday": [["9:00","10:00"]]}}
          IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS RULE: If the text says someone "would rather not meet on X", "avoid more meetings on X", or "can not meet on X", YOU MUST ADD [["0:00", "23:59"]] for day X in their schedule. 
          If it says "after HH:MM", add [["HH:MM", "23:59"]]. If "before HH:MM", add [["0:00", "HH:MM"]]. 
          Pay close attention to lists of days (e.g., "avoid ... on Monday. Tuesday. Wednesday." means block ALL THREE days).

        Use 24-hour format. For participants with wide-open calendars, use empty lists.

    Example:
        >>> parse_schedules("Schedule a meeting for Alice and Bob for 30 min on Monday or Tuesday between 9:00 and 17:00. Alice is busy Monday 9:00-10:00. Bob has no meetings. Bob would rather not meet on Tuesday. Alice avoid more meetings on Monday after 15:00.")
